findings.description: User-controlled display_name and action are interpolated into HTML rows via f-strings with no escaping, allowing stored XSS in the admin dashboard.
The caller-supplied title is injected into <title>/<h1> via str.format without HTML-escaping, enabling XSS if title is attacker-influenced.
The docstring promises an inclusive [start, end] range but the SQL uses created_at < ? (exclusive), so events on the end boundary day are dropped.
save_report writes to an unvalidated out_path, permitting path traversal or arbitrary file overwrite if the path is externally influenced.
Building the rows HTML via repeated string concatenation in a loop is inefficient for large reports; a list join would be better.
Every public method opens and closes its own SQLite connection, adding per-call connection overhead with no pooling or reuse.
Most public methods (render_activity_report, save_report, top_users, event_count) lack docstrings documenting their behavior and parameters.
event_count assumes fetchone() returns a non-None row keyed 'n'; this holds for COUNT(*) but is an unguarded assumption.
